narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.10 and plans to attend law school full-time, your profile shows determination to pursue a legal career, but the admissions model focused on key predictors of bar exam success has flagged some concerns. The model predicts a 63.3% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which is high enough that the university rejected your application to prioritize students with stronger projected outcomes. This decision hinged most on your academic decile rankings from earlier years in school, particularly your Year 3 performance, which landed you in the bottom decile—far below the typical student's mid-range standing. That low Year 3 decile was the biggest factor pushing the prediction toward failure, as it signals to the model a potential struggle with the rigorous demands ahead. Your Year 1 decile, also in the bottom rung compared to the average around the middle of the pack, reinforced this concern and was the second strongest influence in the same direction. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA provided the primary counterbalance, pulling the prediction somewhat toward passing since it's close to the typical 3.21 average for admitted students. Other elements, like your lower-middle family income bracket or LSAT score of 28—which is notably below the usual 36.7—played supporting roles but weren't the top drivers here. In comparison to typical students who pass the bar, your early academic deciles stand out as weaker, while full-time status aligns with the vast majority who succeed. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male from a modest background; it's the model highlighting patterns from past bar-takers where low early deciles often predict tougher roads ahead. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, seeking targeted tutoring to improve academic deciles in any remaining coursework, or applying to programs with bridge courses that support students with similar profiles. Building a stronger narrative around your resilience—perhaps through additional test prep or relevant experience—could help in reapplying elsewhere. Many students in your shoes have turned things around with focused effort, and you're well-positioned to do the same.
